返回本批次

我在每月 20 美元的技术堆栈上运营着多家 MRR 为 10,000 美元的公司

I run multiple $10K MRR companies on a $20/month tech stack

stevehanov.ca 作者 tradertef 分数 60 评论 23 2026/04/12 14:00
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 昨晚，我又被拒绝参加推介夜。这只是预面试，问题不在于我的产品。我已经有MRR了。我已经有每天依赖它的用户。反馈很简单：“你还需要资金做什么？”当我尝试发展自己的想法时，我一次又一次听到这样的说法。精益跑步是我的基因。我构建了您可能使用过的工具，例如 websequencediagrams.com，以及您可能没有使用过的利基产品，例如 eh-trade.ca。对效率的痴迷会带来成功的引导，老实说，很多风险投资人讨厌这一点。将成本保持在接近于零的水平，可以让你获得与以巨大的烧钱率获得一百万美元资金完全相同的跑道。它的压力更小，它使您的架构变得异常简单，并且让您有足够的时间来找到产品与市场的契合度，而无需承受董事会的压力。如果您厌倦了现代的“企业”样板，这里是我如何建立几乎没有任何资源的公司的确切剧本。使用精益服务器 在 2026 年启动 Web 应用程序的简单方法是启动 AWS、配置 EKS 集群、设置 RDS 实例、配置 NAT 网关，然后在单个用户查看您的登陆页面之前每月意外花费 300 美元。明智的方法是租用一台虚拟专用服务器（VPS）。我做的第一件事就是买一个便宜、可靠的盒子。忘记AWS吧。你不会需要它，他们的控制面板是一个迷宫，旨在提取计费升级。我使用 Linode 或 DigitalOcean。每月支付不超过 5 至 10 美元。对于现代 Web 开发人员来说，1GB 的 RAM 听起来很可怕，但如果您知道自己在做什么，那么它就足够了。如果您需要一点喘息空间，只需使用交换文件即可。
  2. 目标是服务请求，而不是维护基础设施。当您拥有一台服务器时，您可以确切地知道日志的位置、崩溃的确切原因以及如何重新启动它。使用精益语言 现在你有限制。你只有千兆字节的内存。您可以运行 Python 或 Ruby 作为主要后端语言，但为什么要这样做呢？您将花费一半的 RAM 来启动解释器和管理 Gunicorn 工作人员。我用 Go 编写后端。 Go 对于 Web 任务的性能要高得多，它是严格类型化的，并且对于 2026 年至关重要的是，对于法学硕士来说，它非常容易推理。但 Go 真正的魔力在于部署过程。没有 pip install 依赖地狱。没有虚拟环境。您可以在笔记本电脑上将整个应用程序编译成一个静态链接的二进制文件，将其 scp 到您的 5 美元服务器上，然后运行它。这是 Go 中完整的、可用于生产的 Web 服务器的样子。不需要臃肿的框架：package main
  3. 导入（“fmt”“net/http”）
  4. func main() { http.HandleFunc("/", func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { fmt.Fprintf(w, "Hello, your MRR is safe here.") }) // 这将轻松处理土豆上每秒 10,000 个请求。 http.ListenAndServe(":8080", nil) }
  5. 使用本地 AI 执行长时间运行的任务如果您家中某处有显卡，则您已经拥有无限的 AI 积分。当我建立 eh-trade.ca 时，我遇到了一个具体问题：我需要对数千家公司进行深入、定性的股票市场研究，总结大量的季度报告。最简单的解决方案是将所有这些都扔到 OpenAI API 处。我本可以支付数百美元的 API 积分，却发现我的提示循环中存在逻辑错误，需要我重新运行整个批次。相反，我在从 Facebook Marketplace 购买的一块布满灰尘的 900 美元显卡（带有 24GB VRAM 的 RTX 3090）上运行 VLLM。当然，这是一项前期投资，但我再也不用向人工智能提供商支付批处理费用了。对于本地人工智能，你有一个独特的升级路径：

Original Summary

  1. Last night, I was rejected from yet another pitch night. It was just the pre-interview, and the problem wasn't my product. I already have MRR. I already have users who depend on it every day. The feedback was simply: "What do you even need funding for?" I hear this time and time again when I try to grow my ideas. Running lean is in my DNA. I've built tools you might have used, like websequencediagrams.com, and niche products you probably haven't, like eh-trade.ca. That obsession with efficiency leads to successful bootstrapping, and honestly, a lot of VCs hate that. Keeping costs near zero gives you the exact same runway as getting a million dollars in funding with a massive burn rate. It's less stressful, it keeps your architecture incredibly simple, and it gives you adequate time to find product-market fit without the pressure of a board breathing down your neck. If you are tired of the modern "Enterprise" boilerplate, here is the exact playbook of how I build my companies to run on nearly nothing. Use a lean server The naive way to launch a web app in 2026 is to fire up AWS, provision an EKS cluster, set up an RDS instance, configure a NAT Gateway, and accidentally spend $300 a month before a single user has even looked at your landing page. The smart way is to rent a single Virtual Private Server (VPS). First thing I do is get a cheap, reliable box. Forget AWS. You aren't going to need it, and their control panel is a labyrinth designed to extract billing upgrades. I use Linode or DigitalOcean. Pay no more than $5 to $10 a month. 1GB of RAM sounds terrifying to modern web developers, but it is plenty if you know what you are doing. If you need a little breathing room, just use a swapfile.
  2. The goal is to serve requests, not to maintain infrastructure. When you have one server, you know exactly where the logs are, exactly why it crashed, and exactly how to restart it. Use a lean language Now you have constraints. You only have a gigabyte of memory. You could run Python or Ruby as your main backend language—but why would you? You'll spend half your RAM just booting the interpreter and managing gunicorn workers. I write my backends in Go. Go is infinitely more performant for web tasks, it's strictly typed, and—crucially for 2026—it is incredibly easy for LLMs to reason about. But the real magic of Go is the deployment process. There is no pip install dependency hell. There is no virtual environment. You compile your entire application into a single, statically linked binary on your laptop, scp it to your $5 server, and run it. Here is what a complete, production-ready web server looks like in Go. No bloated frameworks required: package main
  3. import ( "fmt" "net/http" )
  4. func main() { http.HandleFunc("/", func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { fmt.Fprintf(w, "Hello, your MRR is safe here.") }) // This will comfortably handle 10,000s of requests per second // on a potato. http.ListenAndServe(":8080", nil) }
  5. Use Local AI for long-running tasks If you have a graphics card sitting somewhere in your house, you already have unlimited AI credits. When I was building eh-trade.ca, I had a specific problem: I needed to perform deep, qualitative stock market research on thousands of companies, summarizing massive quarterly reports. The naive solution is to throw all of this at the OpenAI API. I could have paid hundreds of dollars in API credits, only to find a logic bug in my prompt loop that required me to run the whole batch over again. Instead, I'm running VLLM on a dusty $900 graphics card (an RTX 3090 with 24GB of VRAM) I bought off Facebook Marketplace. It’s an upfront investment, sure, but I never have to pay a toll to an AI provider for batch processing again. For local AI, you have a distinct upgrade path:

vxsz 2026/04/12 15:52
我什么也没学到。其中大部分似乎都是常见的基本建议，包含在人工智能编写的段落中......

最初从标题来看，我认为这将是关于集思广益和推出一个成功的想法之类的事情。

I learned nothing. Most of this seems like common basic advice, wrapped up in AI written paragraphs...

Initially from the title, I thought it would be about brainstorming and launching a successful idea, and that sort of thing.

gobdovan 2026/04/12 16:00

Usually when there's "on a [low] $/mo" you'll hear basic advice. You'd be surprised to find out many folks are not aware of this!
在 HN 中查看
thibaultmol 2026/04/12 16:03
很确定这只是由 AI 编写的...否则为什么会有人称之为“Sonnet 3.5 Sonnet 和 gpt 4o”高端型号。
Pretty sure this is just written by AI... Why else would someone call "Sonnet 3.5 Sonnet and gpt 4o' high end models.
在 HN 中查看
gobdovan 2026/04/12 15:58
不错的清单！我想说带有 WAL 的 SQLite 是所提到的最省钱的方法。

需要注意的是：您绝对可以使用 Python 或 Node，就像使用 Go 一样。 Hetzner 提供 4GB RAM、10TB 网络（然后 1 美元/TB 出口）、2CPU 机器，价格为 5 美元。

VPS 的两个免责声明：

如果您使用专用服务器而不是云服务器，请不要忘记经常将数据库备份到存储盒（1TB 为 3 美元/月，使用 rsync）。无论哪种方式，这都是一个很好的做法，但云实例对于硬件故障似乎更可靠。还要避免他们的对象存储。

您负责安全。我看到优秀的开发人员跳过基本的 SSH 强化并在 1 小时内被机器人感染。当我启动服务器时，我的首选动作是两阶段 Terraform 设置：首先，我设置仅允许我的 IP 的 SSH，设置 Tailscale，然后完全关闭公共 SSH IP 入口点。

保重并玩得开心！

Nice list! I'd say the SQLite with WAL is the biggest money saver mentioned.

One note: you can absolutely use Python or Node just as well as Go. There's Hetzner that offers 4GB RAM, 10TB network (then 1$/TB egress), 2CPUs machines for 5$.

Two disclaimers for VPS:

If you're using a dedicated server instead of a cloud server, just don't forget to backup DB to a Storage box often (3$ /mo for 1TB, use rsync). It's a good practice either way, but cloud instances seem more reliable to hardware faults. Also avoid their object store.

You are responsible for security. I saw good devs skipping basic SSH hardening and get infected by bots in <1hr. My go-to move when I spin up servers is a two-stage Terraform setup: first, I set up SSH with only my IP allowed, set up Tailscale and then shutdown the public SSH IP entrypoint completely.

Take care and have fun!

在 HN 中查看
firefoxd 2026/04/12 15:59
我最近正在写这篇文章[0]。在 2000 年代，我们吹嘘我们的服务现在有多便宜。如今，一名有想法的毕业生在享受学生折扣后，在 AWS 中支付了 200 美元。在测试这个想法之前，他们已经倾家荡产，甚至破产了。如今，编程实际上是免费的。

[0]: https://idiallo.com/blog/programming-tools-are-free

I was writing about this recently [0]. In the 2000s, we were bragging about how cheap our services are and are getting. Today, a graduate with an idea is paying $200 amounts in AWS after the student discounts. They break the bank and go broke before they have tested the idea. Programming is literally free today.

[0]: https://idiallo.com/blog/programming-tools-are-free

Just in case, if there are others like me who where wondering what does "MRR" means, it seems to be "monthly recurring revenue".

Not everybody who reads HN is well versed in business/entrepreneur oriented jagon.
在 HN 中查看
weird-eye-issue 2026/04/12 16:04
是的。清楚地。但你真的失去了讽刺意义吗？
Yes. Clearly. But is the irony really lost on you?
在 HN 中查看
hackingonempty 2026/04/12 15:32
> 如果您需要一点喘息空间，只需使用交换文件即可。

即使您不想要任何交换，您也应该始终使用交换文件/分区。 这是因为总是存在冷页，如果没有交换空间，内存就不能用于应用程序或缓冲区，那就浪费了。

> If you need a little breathing room, just use a swapfile.

You should always use a swap file/partition, even if you don't want any swapping. That's because there are always cold pages and if you have no swap space that memory cannot be used for apps or buffers, it's just wasted.

在 HN 中查看
berkes 2026/04/12 15:58
我一直认为我必须添加一个交换文件以避免 OOM 崩溃。我没有意识到上面有冷页面。

有时这种崩溃正是我想要的：在系统中运行一个（微）服务的专用服务器将在此类崩溃时重新启动新服务器（例如类似 Kubernetes）。我宁愿让它立即崩溃，也不愿在降级状态下继续运行。

但在像 OP 节目这样的共享设置或旧的 LAMP-on-a-vps 上，我希望系统开始交换并有机会恢复。 IME 经常这样做。需要几分钟（接近停机时间），但可以更轻松地避免数据损坏或崩溃循环。

基本上，让 Linux 处理恢复与让监控系统处理恢复

I always thought I had to add a swap file to avoid crashing with OOM. I wasn't aware of the cold pages overhead.

Sometimes that crashing is what I want: a dedicated server running one (micro)service in a system that'll restart new servers on such crashes (e.g. Kubernetes-alike). I'd rather have it crash immediately rather than chugging along in degraded state.

But on a shared setup like OP shows, or the old LAMP-on-a-vps, i'd prefer the system to start swapping and have a chance to recover. IME it quite often does. Will take a few minutes (of near downtime) but will avoid data corruption or crash-loops much easier.

Basically, letting Linux handle recovery vs letting a monitoring system handle recovery

在 HN 中查看
dnnddidiej 2026/04/12 15:56
投资者的资金流向是基础设施吗？我以为工资就是这样。也许是营销成本。

因此，投资者应该喜欢廉价的投资理念，因为更多的钱会花在扩大收入的事情上。

如果廉价的基础设施总拥有成本（TCO）和员工时间较高，则例外。

Is infra where investors money is going? I imagined salaries would be it. Marketing costs maybe.

So an investor should love cheap infrs for an idea as more money goes on things that scale the revenue.

Exception would be if the cheap infra TCO with staff time is higher.

brador 2026/04/12 15:53
你已经拥有并拥有将业务规模扩大到最大所需的一切，但这还没有发生，所以更多的钱也无济于事。

你想让VC做什么？

你没有带来计划。

You already have and had everything you need to scale the business to max and it hasn’t happened so more money won’t help.

What do you want VC to do?

You didn’t bring a plan.

在 HN 中查看
tradertef 2026/04/12 14:00
不是我的网站。我发现这很有趣。
Not my website. I found this interesting.
在 HN 中查看
codemog 2026/04/12 15:34
其中很多建议都很好，或者至少很有趣。其中很多都是值得怀疑的。 Python 完全适合后端。使用 SQLite 作为产品数据库是一个坏主意，只需使用 Postgres 或类似的数据库即可。
A lot of this advice is good or at least interesting. A lot of it is questionable. Python is completely fine for the backend. And using SQLite for your prod database is a bad idea, just use Postgres or similar.
在 HN 中查看
tkcranny 2026/04/12 16:02
关于他的 go for simple 方法有很多值得说的。 Python 需要虚拟环境、包管理器、磁盘依赖项、wsgi/asgi 服务器来运行服务器的分叉副本，所有这些都使用 go 的 4 倍到 20 倍的内存使用量。 Docker 通常会在这方面介入，在你意识到之前，你已经陷入了 Helm 图表中，并咒骂 EKS 集群中的 CNI 配置。

go 相当于将一个文件复制到服务器并重新启动其进程，这具有很大的吸引力，并且显然对他来说效果很好。

There’s a lot to be said about his approach with go for simplicity. Python needs virtual environments, package managers, dependencies on disk, a wsgi/asgi server to run forked copies of the server, and all of that uses 4x-20x the ram usage of go. Docker usually gets involved around here and before you know it you’re neck deep in helm charts and cursing CNI configs in an EKS cluster.

The go equivalent of just coping one file across to a server a restarting its process has a lot of appeal and clearly works well for him.

在 HN 中查看
gls2ro 2026/04/12 15:40
为什么 SQLite 不适合生产数据库？

是的，它有一些行为与 PostgreSQL 不同，但我很好奇你为什么这么想。

Why is SQLite bad for production database?

Yes, it has some things that behave differently than PostgreSQL but I am curious about why you think that.

在 HN 中查看
cenamus 2026/04/12 15:53
我认为关键在于，你的 Python Web 应用程序在扩展到 5 美元 VPS 上的 10,000 个客户时会遇到更多问题。当然，您总是可以获得更强大的服务器，但这对每个项目来说都是累加的
I think the point is that your Python webapp will have more problems scaling to let's say 10,000 customers on a 5$ VPS tham Go. Of course you can always get beefier servers, but then that adds up for every project
在 HN 中查看
chrismorgan 2026/04/12 15:49
Python 会带你走很长的路，但它的上限（典型的和绝对的）是 远的 低于 Go 和 Rust 等。对于典型的实现，差异可能是十倍。对于（两者）的仔细实现，它可能远不止于此。

区别重要吗？你必须做出这样的决定。

至于你对 SQLite 的排斥：请证明为什么这是一个坏主意。因为我强烈不同意。

Python will take you a long way, but its ceiling (both typical and absolute) is far lower than the likes of Go and Rust. For typical implementations, the difference may be a factor of ten. For careful implementations (of both), it can be a lot more than that.

Does the difference matter? You must decide that.

As for your dismissing SQLite: please justify why it’s a bad idea. Because I strongly disagree.

PhilippGille 2026/04/12 15:49
他特别提到，他使用 GitHub Copilot 是因为 Microsoft 按请求而不是令牌计费。
He specifically mentions that he is using GitHub Copilot because of how Microsoft bills per request instead of token.
